The US-based producer said its outlook matches the current market range, easing worries of a sharper slowdown.  
Investors watched closely for any changes tied to pricing, processing volumes, and customer restocking cycles.  
The company’s guidance also reflects the continuing cost pressure from refining and logistics, where margins can swing quickly.  
Rare-earth supply chains remain a focus as manufacturers balance security of supply with inventory levels.  
Shares moved in line with the update, as traders weigh longer-term demand from magnets used in electrification and defense.
bodies_by_headline_hash.ee5fd84d6d977383869f:
A rare-earth miner reported steady production guidance for the coming period, citing continued performance from its producing assets and stable operating conditions, according to a company update.

The company said it expects production to remain consistent with previously communicated levels, supported by ongoing ramp-up activities and normalised throughput across its processing facilities. It did not provide a major change to its outlook, instead reaffirming its guidance framework and the key assumptions used to forecast output, including planned production schedules, feedstock availability and recoveries at its separation plant.

In the update, the miner also outlined progress on operational reliability, including maintenance planning and equipment uptime targets. Management said the company is monitoring critical stages in the separation process and has maintained process controls designed to limit variation in product quality and yield. The firm indicated that it remains on track to meet its internal targets for feed handling and processing efficiency, which it said underpin the guidance.

On the market side, the company did not announce new offtake contracts or changes to pricing arrangements, but it referenced the continued demand environment for specific rare-earth products used in magnets and industrial applications. It said customer requirements and production planning remain aligned for the near term, with shipments scheduled based on current purchase orders and typical lead times.

A spokesperson said in a statement that “our operations have been steady and we are comfortable with the guidance range as we continue to manage feed supply and plant recoveries.” The company added that it is focused on maintaining throughput while completing ongoing reliability works that are scheduled to minimise disruption to production.

Separately, the miner disclosed routine updates on costs and capital spending, noting that near-term expenditures are tracking expectations. It said any variability would be driven primarily by standard factors such as energy prices, reagent consumption and logistics, rather than by changes in core production assumptions. The firm did not forecast a shift in cash generation, but it indicated that guidance is based on current cost parameters and production plans.

The update comes as rare-earth producers continue to balance supply stability with the long lead times involved in mine development and rare-earth separation capacity. Investors and downstream manufacturers have also focused on the ability of suppliers to deliver consistent magnet-grade material amid fluctuating demand across end markets.
bodies_by_headline_hash.11c15bbd2da5c310a485: Rare-earth exchange-traded funds recorded marginally positive inflows in month-to-date trading through March 10, according to data compiled from fund flows. Aggregate net purchases totaled about $12 million versus a net outflow of roughly $45 million reported for the comparable year-ago period, with the average daily inflow near $1.2 million over the first 10 trading days. Holdings were little changed as managers shifted some positions within the sector, with the largest movements concentrated in proxy rare-earth baskets tied to NdPr and magnet-related names. Total assets under management for the group rose by approximately 0.4% to about $3.1 billion, reflecting inflows rather than broad price gains. Performance remained mixed, with several underlying miners and refiners showing single-digit declines from early February levels despite steadier spot pricing for select rare-earth oxides. Analysts attributed the muted demand to cautious risk positioning ahead of late-March policy and trade updates affecting critical-mineral supply chains.
bodies_by_headline_hash.16df6fa026a3beac3c07:
A debate over how to model rare-earth demand took center stage at a mining conference this week.  
Executives and researchers argued that forecasts diverge because of different assumptions on EV, wind, and defense procurement timelines.  
Some analysts warned that short-term data can overstate long-run consumption, especially when recycling and substitution plans are underweighted.  
Others said models must reflect fast-moving supply chain commitments, including new magnet and refining capacity already in construction.  
The discussion also turned to how quickly prices transmit to buying decisions for high-grade materials.  
Participants agreed on one point: demand uncertainty is now as important as deposit quality.
bodies_by_headline_hash.bd038a02e1206883a37f:
A rare-earths producer held an investor day on Wednesday and restated its long-term targets, underscoring expectations for demand growth in the sector used in clean energy and defence applications.

The company said it is maintaining its outlook for future production and margins as it continues expanding processing capacity and securing additional feedstock contracts. Management presented updated milestones for downstream processing and said it remains focused on improving cost efficiency across its supply chain, including conversion and separation steps.

At the event, the firm reiterated longer-term targets for annual output and operating performance, which it said are supported by signed and contracted offtake agreements as well as bids in active procurement channels. It also highlighted progress on projects aimed at strengthening supply reliability for magnet and alloy customers, a segment it said is increasingly prioritising traceability and consistent specifications.

Investors were provided with a breakdown of the company’s development plan by business line, including steps to scale rare-earth separation capacity and to expand processing of mixed rare-earth materials into products demanded by original equipment manufacturers. The company said it is targeting a ramp-up that will be phased to align with customer qualification timelines, while limiting capital intensity during near-term periods.

Chief executive officer Marcos Valdez said the restated targets reflect “our current project execution pace and the contracted demand we see over the coming years,” adding that the company expects to benefit from higher utilisation as expansions come online.

The investor day comes as the rare-earth market has seen heightened attention from governments and manufacturers seeking to reduce dependence on concentrated sources of supply. Prices for some rare-earth products have been volatile in recent years, influenced by Chinese production dynamics, purchasing patterns from magnet makers and the timing of new capacity. Companies across the industry have also faced shifting costs for energy, shipping and reagents used in separation.

In its presentation, the company did not provide new near-term financial guidance but described longer-term assumptions around volume growth, product mix and operational efficiencies. Analysts said the reaffirmation of targets is likely to be monitored alongside progress on capacity additions and the pace at which customer demand converts into binding offtake.
bodies_by_headline_hash.ddbad97294b773014d2d: A new scholarship fund targeting rare-earth metallurgy students was launched on 12 March 2026, aiming to support training in processing, magnet materials and separation chemistry. The fund will award 120 stipends over the next four years, with grants ranging from $2,000 to $6,500 each, and is designed to cover tuition, lab fees and basic research materials for undergraduate and master’s students. Eligibility will be limited to applicants enrolled in metallurgy, materials science or chemical engineering tracks with coursework in inorganic synthesis or extractive metallurgy, according to the organizers. Priority will be given to applicants who can demonstrate prior coursework or internships tied to rare-earth mining, solvent extraction or recycling of NdFeB magnets. Selection will be based on academic performance, a short technical plan for a capstone project and a commitment to work in the supply chain for at least 18 months after graduation. The program also plans to match top recipients with partner labs starting in September 2026, when the first cohort begins new research rotations.
bodies_by_headline_hash.3d101b91245dde795e54:
A rare-earth trade mission joined officials and companies at a Tokyo summit focused on critical minerals.  
The meeting aimed to deepen supply links for elements used in magnets, batteries, and defense technologies.  
Participants discussed investment pathways, offtake agreements, and long-term procurement to reduce disruptions.  
Tokyo hosted conversations on building processing capacity beyond mining, including separation and refining.  
Delegations also reviewed standards for responsible sourcing and traceability across the supply chain.  
The mission highlighted partnerships between government agencies and private firms in Japan and partner countries.  
No single timeline was announced, but attendees signaled an intent to coordinate next steps later this year.
bodies_by_headline_hash.f381bd298a2e28d85d10:
A panel at a rare-earth conference on Friday focused on the economics of recycling the materials used in magnets and other clean-energy technologies, highlighting how collection systems and processing costs shape whether recovered feedstock can compete with new supply.

Speakers said recycling can reduce both environmental impacts and supply risks, but only if it achieves scale and stable pricing for mixed rare-earth streams. The panelists discussed how recyclers are balancing the cost of collecting end-of-life products such as electric vehicle motors and wind turbine components against the expense of separating and refining rare-earth elements to meet market specifications.

One challenge raised during the session was that scrap arriving at recycling plants is often heterogeneous, containing varying rare-earth compositions and contaminants. That variability, participants said, can increase the cost of hydrometallurgical or separation steps and complicate qualification by magnet producers and other industrial customers. Several speakers pointed to design changes and better material tracing as ways to improve the economics of feedstock.

“Recycling economics depend less on the technology alone and more on the supply chain—how much scrap is available, what it contains, and whether downstream buyers can use the recovered material without additional processing,” one industry analyst said.

Panelists also examined the price dynamics of rare earths, which can fluctuate widely. When prices fall, recycling margins can compress, discouraging investment in new capacity or feedstock contracts. Conversely, higher prices for dysprosium, terbium, and other heavy rare earths can make recovery more attractive, but only if producers can ensure consistent outputs of those specific elements rather than broadly mixed concentrates.

The discussion included the role of policy and finance in de-risking recycling investments, particularly for facilities that require upfront capital for separation and refining equipment. Speakers said extended producer responsibility schemes, procurement programs for recycled content, and harmonized reporting standards could help create longer-term demand signals.

Participants also touched on emerging processing routes, including approaches aimed at improving recovery yields and reducing chemical usage. Several speakers said the biggest near-term gains may come from optimizing existing flows of industrial scrap—such as manufacturing waste from magnet and electronics production—before expanding into more complex end-of-life streams.

The panel concluded that while recycling is technically feasible, making it economically competitive will require coordinated efforts across collection, sorting, refining, and offtake agreements.
bodies_by_headline_hash.8a11744d455222e7be33: A rare-earth producer has renewed an offtake agreement with an Asian magnet manufacturer, extending supplies of key magnet feedstocks for electric motors used in appliances and vehicles. The company, which did not specify pricing in its announcement, said the renewed contract covers shipments over a multi-year period starting in 2026, with delivery schedules aligned to quarterly production plans at the customer’s plants. The producer will supply separated rare earths used in permanent magnets, including neodymium and dysprosium grades, which are typically sourced in tight global markets. Both parties said the renewal includes provisions to adjust volumes based on demand and furnace capacity, with minimum purchase commitments and performance-linked penalties. The deal follows a broader reassessment of sourcing risk in Asia after 2024–2025 price swings tied to export curbs and refinery outages. Industry data show Asian magnet makers have been working to secure stable feedstock inflows ahead of stricter procurement rules and rising uptake in hybrid and electric drive systems.
bodies_by_headline_hash.fe7add89b27227f22d4a:
A rare-earth industry lobby group has released new policy recommendations aimed at reducing supply risks for critical minerals.  
The group calls for faster permitting, more stable government procurement, and targeted financing for mining and processing.  
It argues that domestic and allied production must expand to meet demand from magnets used in EVs, wind turbines, and defense systems.  
The recommendations also urge greater recycling incentives and support for separation and refining capacity, not just extraction.  
The document highlights trade alignment with partner countries to avoid sudden export restrictions and to strengthen traceability standards.  
It said near-term planning should focus on bottleneck facilities such as separation plants.
bodies_by_headline_hash.af123cd9e574cfd08575:
A rarely used technical snapshot for global rare-earth markets showed prices tracking their 90-day moving averages in a chart circulated on March 14, highlighting the short-term trend of key metals used in magnets and electronics.

The chart, shared via the Telegram channel Rare Earth Watch, presents a rolling 90-day average for several widely followed rare-earth products, a method intended to smooth day-to-day volatility and make the direction of the market easier to gauge. The update does not introduce new fundamentals such as mine supply changes or policy measures, but it is positioned as a reference point for traders monitoring momentum in a sector sensitive to shifting demand and production disruptions.

Across the figures shown, the 90-day averages for select light and heavy rare-earths move in tandem with recent spot swings, with most series appearing to be in a gradual phase of adjustment rather than a sharp reversal. The chart’s emphasis is on whether the averages are rising or falling versus recent weeks, a common approach for market participants evaluating whether prices are likely to continue trending or revert.

Market analysts have said the rare-earth sector can react quickly to logistical disruptions, China’s export enforcement and pricing guidance, and shifts in inventories held by processors and manufacturers. The metals are also closely tied to demand for permanent magnets used in electric vehicles, wind turbines and industrial motors, which can change with contract cycles and procurement timing.

In the Telegram post, Rare Earth Watch directed viewers to “the 90-day moving average” as the day’s indicator, suggesting that the chart should be read alongside broader market information rather than as a standalone valuation tool. The channel did not provide detailed price levels for each item within the shared image, and it did not cite specific data sources or methodology beyond the moving-average framing.

Traders and investors often rely on moving averages because rare-earth prices can be distorted by intermittent availability of material, contract renegotiations, and varying liquidity across individual compounds. While the update is technical in nature, it reflects ongoing monitoring of trend direction in a market where pricing moves can carry through to magnet supply chains and downstream manufacturers.

The latest chart was posted on March 14, with no immediate accompanying commentary on changes to production, shipping costs or export policy.
bodies_by_headline_hash.b2feb2d2478dba634ca8: Rare-earth supply monitoring data released March 15, 2026 indicates unusual congestion in dysprosium (Dy) and related heavy rare-earth receiving routes, following what analysts describe as a typical post-adjustment spread pattern. The dataset, compiled from shipping and market-access observations across Asia and Europe, shows DR-linked deliveries slowed across multiple nodes during the first 10 days of March, with congestion indices peaking on March 7 before easing by March 12. Compared with the same interval in February 2026, the weighted delay widened by an estimated 18–24 hours per consignment, while average transit variability rose to roughly 1.6× normal. Spot pricing for Dy-bearing products moved within a narrower band than in prior congestion events, suggesting demand did not shift as sharply as logistics. For March 13–15, the spread narrowed again, returning to levels similar to the late-February baseline. The monitoring group cited no single disruption event, attributing the pattern to synchronized capacity constraints at common consolidation hubs and seasonal schedule compression.
bodies_by_headline_hash.f0791a6381ffc067f559:
Rare-earth volumes in London Metal Exchange warehouses stayed steady in the latest update.  
Inventories did not show a meaningful shift across the tracked rare-earth product mix.  
Traders pointed to balanced inflows and withdrawals as the main reason for the flat reading.  
That stability suggests near-term logistics and sourcing remain predictable for warehouse-linked buyers.  
Market participants will be watching for any change tied to contract cycles or regional shipment schedules.  
For now, the data indicates limited immediate pressure on physical availability.  
Sentiment in the complex may therefore remain focused on broader policy and demand signals rather than stockpiles.
bodies_by_headline_hash.866a7a423114aff19547:
A post shared by an account tracking rare-earth developments on Telegram offered what it described as routine market commentary, drawing attention from traders and observers monitoring supply-chain signals in the sector.

The message, posted on March 15 and attributed to the “Rare Earth Watch” feed, did not announce any new policy measures or company actions. Instead, it summarized recent market movements and reiterated common themes in the rare-earth trade, including pricing dynamics for key materials used in magnets and electronics, as well as ongoing shifts in demand patterns across manufacturing industries.

According to the post, the market continues to be influenced by a mix of spot activity and longer-term contract expectations, with buyers weighing delivery timelines and potential changes in availability. It also pointed to the sensitivity of rare-earth pricing to announcements related to refining capacity and magnet production, where lead times can span months and affect downstream orders.

The account said it was monitoring “routine” developments rather than reacting to a specific catalyst, and it urged readers to treat the commentary as a snapshot of conditions rather than a forecast. Traders following the feed said the distinction matters because rare-earth prices can move quickly when unexpected supply interruptions, export controls, or major procurement decisions are reported.

In comments accompanying the post, the account highlighted that sentiment in rare-earth markets often shifts on incremental information—such as updates on inventory levels, customer restocking cycles, or changes in the pace of shipments—rather than on single headlines. It also referenced how volatility in related commodities and energy costs can feed through to rare-earth processing costs, affecting contract negotiations.

Market participants said the Telegram feed’s observations are part of a broader ecosystem of surveillance used by investors who track rare-earth supply and demand. Many in the sector routinely scan official releases, shipping data and corporate filings alongside social-media commentary to assemble a working picture of conditions.

While the post did not cite new data sources or provide figures, it was widely re-shared among accounts that aggregate rare-earth news and commentary. The latest message did not indicate that any regulatory action or corporate restructuring was imminent, leaving traders to continue balancing near-term market moves against longer-term capacity and demand considerations.
bodies_by_headline_hash.2e468ce1f6ff080e9fac: Rare-earth pricing showed little month-on-month movement in mid-March 2026, according to a market note circulated on 15 March. The overall price delta across tracked neodymium-praseodymium, dysprosium, and terbium was described as marginal versus the February 2026 baseline. Neodymium-praseodymium remained largely flat, with changes in the monthly range that analysts said were within routine bid-ask and contract-cycle variation. Dysprosium and terbium followed the same pattern, with measured shifts too small to materially alter procurement budgets for buyers resetting quarterly orders. The note attributed the subdued movement to steady spot liquidity and limited evidence of new supply disruptions since early February 2026. It also pointed to demand pacing tied to equipment restocking and distributor inventories, rather than a surge in end-user offtake. Traders said the next meaningful signal is likely to come after late-March contracts settle, when sellers and buyers typically reprice for the second quarter.
bodies_by_headline_hash.57a61e31901a6380deb7:
Lithium spot prices fell about 6% after inventories in China rose.  
Traders pointed to increased warehouse availability that loosened near-term supply tightness.  
The move suggests more material is reaching the market than buyers were bracing for.  
Sentiment weakened as price expectations shifted toward a slower pace of demand-led buying.  
Deal activity reflected that caution, with fewer aggressive bids and more selective purchasing.  
Some sellers lowered offers to clear stock and reduce holding costs.  
Market watchers said the next driver will be whether inventory growth continues or starts to reverse.  
For now, the spot market is signaling softer pricing pressure.
bodies_by_headline_hash.ee013c50b31f6e8dcd57:
Cobalt prices rose as battery makers renewed buying interest for the metal, after a period of cautious procurement, according to market participants.

Dealers said demand tightened in key purchasing windows for cathode production, with several buyers seeking spot and near-term supply to support planned output levels. The renewed interest came as refiners and intermediaries reported more inquiries for available material, including parcels tied to existing contract frameworks and spot nominations for the second quarter.

Fastmarkets and other pricing contributors reported that market liquidity improved modestly compared with the prior session, where price direction had been harder to assess due to thinner trade. Bids and offers were described as more active, and transactions were completed at firmer levels than in recent weeks, reflecting the change in buyer behavior.

Producers and traders pointed to customer requirements for nickel-cobalt-manganese and cobalt-containing lithium-ion chemistries used in electric vehicles and portable devices. While longer-term offtake negotiations continue, buyers have increasingly focused on near-term inventory replenishment amid supply planning for vehicle build schedules and consumer electronics demand.

“Battery customers are looking to top up, and that has pulled more activity into the spot market,” one metals trader said, adding that the firmness in offers was linked to confidence that shortages in specific grades could persist into subsequent deliveries.

Suppliers said they were balancing sales commitments with the need to maintain coverage for contracted obligations, which limited the amount of cobalt available for immediate loading. Some intermediaries also reported that access to particular feedstocks and conversion capacity could affect the timing of refined output, contributing to the tighter tone.

The market has been sensitive to shifts in battery demand expectations and to broader commodity conditions, including movements in related metals used in cathodes. Analysts said that if the buying interest continues, it could support price levels in subsequent assessments, though they cautioned that the trajectory would depend on how quickly new supply enters the market and how stable customer buying remains.

In the background, traders continued to monitor developments in downstream policy and industrial capacity, including announcements related to refining expansion and battery manufacturing schedules. Any change in those areas could influence both spot buying and longer-term contract terms for cobalt, market participants said.
bodies_by_headline_hash.ccc5bc6581d531469c5a: Nickel prices are expected to remain under pressure after the market stayed oversupplied through year-end, extending a surplus cycle that has weighed on inventories and refinery economics. Trade flows into Asia accelerated in the third quarter, while production from major mines in Indonesia and elsewhere continued near nameplate rates, outpacing demand growth from battery supply chains. Refinery output remained steady into 2025, and conversion capacity additions reduced spot tightness across several grades of nickel intermediates. Fastmarkets data showed that warehouse stocks and LME-visible levels rose during the fourth quarter, consistent with a build ahead of end-December contract periods. Market participants said that absorption by stainless and alloy producers was not sufficient to offset higher secondary supply and slower procurement, particularly during November and December. With end-use buying typically seasonal but weaker than usual late in the year, buyers shifted toward shorter restocking windows, limiting support for prompt pricing. The oversupply backdrop is expected to carry into early 2026 unless offtake schedules tighten in the first half and any output disruptions materially reduce available tonnes.
bodies_by_headline_hash.57dd65144433ab6e1ab2:
Copper on the LME is testing the $9,500 level as traders probe a key resistance zone ahead of fresh price signals.  
Momentum has picked up, with buyers showing interest on dips and sellers defending the overhead barrier.  
Market participants are watching for follow-through that would confirm a breakout rather than a temporary push.  
Liquidity remains a focus, since thin order books can exaggerate short-term moves near round-number levels.  
Analysts say near-term direction may hinge on macro sentiment and any new updates on supply risk and demand expectations.  
For now, $9,500 holds as the line traders are trying to cross.
bodies_by_headline_hash.8c1178c548dff083ef06:
The tin market tightened again as deliveries from Indonesia slowed and buyers competed for limited cargoes, traders said on Wednesday. Prices for physical tin firmed on expectations that near-term supply will remain constrained, even as some market participants sought alternatives to cover demand.

Indonesia remains the dominant source of global refined tin, and participants linked the latest tightening to export volumes that have not kept pace with buying interest. Several traders said shipments have faced operational constraints and shifting schedules at producing and trading hubs, affecting the timing of arrivals into key consuming regions.

LME-listed and cash market premiums rose on the expectation that remaining inventory would be absorbed quickly, with buyers placed under pressure to secure metal for immediate delivery. Cash deals for tin were described as firmer, particularly for grades and delivery windows that can be matched with industrial users’ production plans.

In Asia, smelters and traders have been balancing production and logistics as they respond to demand from downstream sectors, including electronics, solder and alloys. One trader said that while demand has been steady, “the bottleneck is on the supply side,” adding that export availability is the main factor influencing the latest price support.

Despite the market’s tightening, traders said activity in forward and paper markets has been more cautious than in prior cycles, reflecting uncertainty around how quickly Indonesia’s export flows may normalize. Some participants pointed to the risk that tightening could ease if additional cargoes arrive sooner than expected, or if buyers shift to alternative procurement channels.

Market watchers said inventories at trading and warehouse locations remain a key signal to follow in the coming weeks. If warehouse stocks continue to fall or remain stable while purchases accelerate, premiums could stay elevated. Conversely, any increase in Indonesian export schedules could reduce urgency and lead to softer assessments of the tightness.

For now, traders said the market’s near-term balance remains sensitive to shipping timing and export decisions out of Indonesia, with buyers continuing to seek cargoes ahead of future sales windows.
bodies_by_headline_hash.9bda5035221d02fe0daf: Graphite suppliers are warning that third-quarter supply tightness is building in the flake market, with availability expected to tighten further through July 2026, according to market participants. Several producers said upstream sorting and processing constraints, combined with higher freight costs since February, are limiting shipments of standard flake grades to industrial buyers. Estimates shared across supplier channels point to reduced spot availability versus contracted volumes for Q3, particularly for medium and large flake sizes commonly used in expandable applications. Buyers are reported to be moving to earlier call-offs and requesting shorter lead times to avoid late-August delivery risk, as inventories at distributors remain thin following the first half drawdown. At the same time, negotiations for new offtake terms are shifting toward tighter delivery windows and revised pricing mechanics, with some contracts referencing monthly indices rather than quarterly averages. The situation is expected to peak in late Q3, while some suppliers indicated incremental tonnage could return in October if processing capacity and port throughput normalize.
bodies_by_headline_hash.8e3004145a47f6723cf8:
Platinum and palladium are moving in different directions as auto demand reshapes expectations for both metals.  
Platinum is gaining support from a shift toward higher loadings of platinum-containing catalysts in certain regions and powertrain mixes.  
Palladium, meanwhile, is facing pressure from continued substitution and optimization by automakers aiming to reduce cost per vehicle produced.  
Market participants are recalibrating forecasts for catalyst consumption rather than treating the two precious metals as tightly linked.  
Traders say the split will likely show up first in physical premiums, then in wider price spreads across exchange benchmarks.  
Attention is now turning to future emissions regulations and production schedules.
bodies_by_headline_hash.63cffde9cbc6b0bff369:
Tungsten spot prices for European buyers moved higher this week as the premium over benchmark levels widened, according to market sources.

The increase reflects tighter availability of prompt material and continued demand from buyers seeking short-notice supply, traders said. Several sellers indicated they were prioritising allocations, which reduced the number of offers available in the spot market and pushed negotiated premiums upward.

One trader said the premium widened because lead times on certain grades remained longer than buyers expected, while restocking demand persisted. “European customers are still competing for immediate quantities, and that keeps the spot premium supported,” the trader said.

Fastmarkets data showed that for spot tungsten, the premium for material sold to Europe over a benchmark reference level expanded compared with the prior assessment. Sources attributed the change to a combination of reduced prompt supply and the timing of procurement cycles, which can concentrate buying ahead of contract re-negotiations.

In addition to spot activity, participants said physical demand for tungsten-bearing inputs continued to be supported by end-use requirements in Europe, although buying remained selective. Traders noted that purchasing decisions are influenced by inventory positions and contract prices, with some buyers using spot deals to cover gaps when longer-term deliveries are delayed.

Market participants also pointed to broader logistics and procurement constraints affecting the flow of material. While transportation conditions have improved in some routes, they said delays and limited warehousing options in certain destinations can still affect how quickly sellers can respond to spot requests.

Suppliers have largely maintained their pricing stance, traders said, citing the need to secure replacement material for spot shipments. Some sellers offered smaller volumes, which in turn reduced liquidity and increased the sensitivity of premiums to each trade.

Overall, the widening premium suggests that prompt tungsten supply available to European buyers remains constrained relative to demand. Market watchers said they will monitor whether improved availability in upcoming weeks narrows the premium or if prompt shortages persist into the next assessment period.
bodies_by_headline_hash.a8822c44ea6375c8fdf0: Vanadium prices stabilised after a first-quarter drawdown as buyers returned to the market following softer offer levels, according to traders monitoring bids and assessments. Over the three months to 31 March, sentiment weakened amid reduced restocking by steelmakers and expectations of tighter supply availability later in 2026. By 18 March, benchmark assessments were largely flat week-on-week, with movement confined to narrow bid-offer ranges rather than broad repricing across major grades. Market participants cited improved liquidity in spot lots and fewer trade cancellations after producers adjusted production schedules and maintenance runs. Inventories at key processors remained the primary swing factor, with some warehouses reporting lower prompt availability even as upstream pricing pressure eased. Analysts said demand support from stainless and hard-facing applications should offset near-term volatility, but volume growth depends on whether Q2 purchasing resumes at prior run-rates, particularly in Asia and Europe.
bodies_by_headline_hash.6981ed8682b1c69294ff:
Gallium markets moved into balance after German scrap shipments resumed, easing supply tightness.  
Fastmarkets said reintroduced flows reduced spot pressure and stabilized availability for processors and refiners.  
Deal activity improved as buyers regained confidence in short-term sourcing.  
Pricing remained firm but showed less upward momentum than in the prior week, reflecting steadier feedstock inflows.  
Market participants noted that scrap volumes can swing quickly with industrial restart schedules and sorting capacity in Europe.  
They expect the next pricing test to come from changes in conversion demand and any renewed disruptions at collection points.  
Overall sentiment shifted from constrained to orderly, with traders watching weekly logistics.
bodies_by_headline_hash.f46eafac7a4901db319b:
Indium prices rose to a two-year high as stronger demand from semiconductor applications tightened supply in the global metals market, according to traders and data providers.

Benchmark indium metal prices increased to the highest level seen in about two years, driven by a rise in purchases tied to electronics production and by lingering constraints in refined output, market participants said. Indium is used in flat-panel displays, semiconductors and touchscreens, and it is also present in certain alloys and coatings, making its supply sensitive to shifts in technology demand.

Several buyers said semiconductor-linked consumption had improved faster than expected, particularly for components used in consumer devices and industrial electronics. Traders also pointed to firming expectations for additional restocking by processors and component makers ahead of the spring production cycle.

On the supply side, refined indium availability remained uneven. While producers have continued to sell from contracted streams, spot availability has been limited by smelter schedules and by the time lag between upstream zinc and tin processing and the subsequent refining of indium. Market participants said that although some sellers have been more active on short-dated contracts, the volume needed to ease tightness has been slow to materialize.

“We’re seeing demand show up in more consistent buying patterns from buyers tied to semiconductor supply chains,” one metals trader said. “That, combined with constrained refined availability, is keeping the market supported.”

The price increase comes as other supply indicators remain mixed. Some producers have reported stable production runs, but traders said that output additions have not been sufficient to offset consumption gains. Inventories held by intermediaries have also been slow to build, according to people familiar with the market, limiting the buffering effect typically seen when prices rise.

Fastmarkets and other industry analysts have linked recent strength in indium to broader semiconductor equipment and component manufacturing trends, alongside a narrower-than-usual balance between production and demand. Any further acceleration in electronics orders could keep upward pressure on prices, while weaker buying or improved supply deliveries would likely cap the rally, traders said.

For now, market participants said the two-year high underscores how quickly indium can react to technology demand given its comparatively concentrated production and refining pathways. They added that pricing could remain volatile as buyers test higher levels and as sellers respond to contract renewals in the coming weeks.
bodies_by_headline_hash.48f69f51f312d47ebb1f: Molybdenum markets held steady after news that a production outage in China had reduced output for several days, according to traders tracking spot and contract negotiations on and after March 18, 2026. Offers for molybdenum concentrate and standard grades were reported unchanged through March 20 despite concerns about near-term supply, with buyers indicating they continued to rely on existing inventory and recently booked cargoes. Chinese suppliers acknowledged intermittent downtime tied to plant maintenance and logistics constraints, but industry participants said the disruption affected only limited tonnage relative to monthly baselines. Fastmarkets data monitors showed that benchmark prices moved within a narrow band rather than repricing, reflecting sparse evidence of physical shortages. Traders pointed to normal import flows into trading hubs and ongoing contract deliveries that were not yet interrupted. One factor cited was that most market participants priced the outage as a short-cycle event, with replenishment expected by late March if utilities and transport schedules return to plan.
bodies_by_headline_hash.0c03b980fe1a412c728e:
Uranium spot prices pushed higher, testing the $110 level as buyers weighed growing supply anxieties.  
Market participants pointed to tighter availability of near-term material, with sellers remaining cautious ahead of upcoming contract coverage.  
The move reflects sensitivity to expectations around production schedules, transport logistics, and the pace of inventory drawdowns.  
Deal flow improved at higher marks, but liquidity stayed selective as utilities and traders sought clarity on term pricing.  
Analysts said the next directional cue will come from additional signals on production restarts and any changes in secondary supplies.  
Until then, spot volatility is likely to remain elevated.
bodies_by_headline_hash.01fc497eb86f0884fe9b:
Cadmium prices were steady on Monday as trading activity remained subdued across a thin market, according to participants and data providers.

Market liquidity stayed limited, with several buyers and sellers reporting fewer counterparty bids than usual. As a result, prices moved little across most assessment ranges, despite periodic inquiry from end-users and traders looking to replenish inventories.

Fastmarkets’ assessment for cadmium metal in a widely tracked format held at steady levels, reflecting the lack of fresh deals large enough to change the benchmark. One dealer said the market was “waiting for clearer demand signals,” noting that movements in finished-battery and coating-related orders have been uneven, which has kept hedging and spot buying cautious.

In Asia, where much of the physical flow for refined metals is concentrated, participants said buyers continued to focus on short-term needs rather than placing larger coverage orders. Sellers, meanwhile, indicated that offer volumes were not heavily pressured, but were also not being expanded aggressively due to the low number of transactions.

In parallel, premiums for refined cadmium and variations tied to delivery terms were reported to remain narrow. Traders said any price adjustments were more likely to come from contract conversions or shipments that settle later in the month, rather than from immediate spot negotiations.

Analysts monitoring cadmium also pointed to the metal’s sensitivity to broader industrial demand, particularly for rechargeable battery supply chains and niche applications such as pigments and electroplating. However, with market activity currently constrained, near-term price discovery has been limited.

Market participants said they expect volumes to improve as more participants return from holiday schedules and as industrial buyers re-engage with procurement cycles. Until then, they said assessments are likely to remain anchored to the most recent settled levels unless a larger-than-usual deal emerges to reset the range.
bodies_by_headline_hash.7e0e5dd0bf482882bc02: Premiums for selenium in the market narrowed on 2026-03-21 as buyers in South Korea reduced procurement amid softer end-demand. Fast-moving offers tightened after trading interest cooled at the start of the week, with pricing shifting toward levels closer to assessments rather than standing at wider spot premiums. Several deals were reported at narrower differentials versus benchmark reference ranges, reflecting a slowdown in import-led buying cycles that typically support premiums during periods of stronger consumption. Suppliers indicated that lead times and allocation terms remained broadly steady, but orders were spread out instead of concentrated on immediate shipment windows. Market participants also pointed to improved availability of material from recent supply arrivals, which reduced the urgency to secure inventories. Overall, selenium premiums narrowed across active lots, while liquidity remained concentrated in near-term contract quantities rather than longer-dated commitments.
bodies_by_headline_hash.62b304590e3e452408c2:
Bismuth prices are set to face fresh pressure after China announced a higher export tax.  
The rate will rise to 10% starting in May, according to industry reporting.  
The change targets outbound shipments and is expected to tighten availability for overseas buyers.  
Export costs will likely feed through into spot pricing for refiners, semiconductor inputs, and chemical grades that use bismuth.  
Traders said the decision could also reduce near-term buying interest ahead of the effective date.  
Market participants will watch whether Chinese producers increase domestic supply to offset reduced export demand.  
Any further policy adjustments could widen price swings across the rare metals complex.
bodies_by_headline_hash.a6a30539c0f5afa48568:
Antimony prices have jumped in recent trading as new and existing sanctions constrained Russian exports of the metal used in flame retardants, batteries and alloys, traders and market sources said.

In Asia and Europe, buyers have shifted orders to alternative suppliers after shipments from Russia slowed, according to shipping and procurement data cited by market participants. Several importers said lead times lengthened and offers for refined antimony became harder to source, lifting benchmark prices over the past two weeks.

Antimony is a critical input for a range of industrial products, and refined material supply is comparatively tight outside major producing countries. Russia has historically been one of the larger exporters of antimony concentrates and metal, and exporters have faced additional compliance checks related to sanctions enforcement, sources said. They added that some customers are requiring more documentation and are reducing contract volumes while they reassess counterparties and logistics routes.

Argus and other market trackers reported higher spot assessments, with increases attributed to both constrained physical availability and rising premiums for prompt delivery. One factor cited was that traders have been reluctant to sell inventory without clearer visibility on onward payment and shipping pathways, adding to the scarcity for immediate tonnage.

A representative for a European distributor said the market “rebalanced quickly” after export constraints tightened, with buyers accepting higher prices to secure deliveries before the next contracting cycle. The distributor, which asked not to be identified because it was not authorized to speak publicly, said deals were increasingly structured around shorter window shipments and tighter quality specifications.

Sanctions have also encouraged some producers and refiners to prioritize domestic contracts or longer-term deals with non-sanctioned counterparties, sources said. Meanwhile, downstream firms have been working through inventories, but procurement desks at some battery and materials manufacturers have started to place earlier orders, anticipating further volatility.

Analysts said whether prices can sustain the rise will depend on the pace of shipments from alternative sources, including China and other producing regions, as well as any further enforcement actions affecting Russia. They added that any easing in export flows would likely reduce premiums for prompt delivery, though it may take time for contracted volumes to realign across the supply chain.
bodies_by_headline_hash.c467440116cb886985d8: A German secondary smelter supporting the processing of germanium feedstocks announced maintenance work that will affect planned output from its facility in the coming weeks, according to an internal notice circulated to customers. The company said the shutdown is scheduled to begin on 2026-03-28 and is expected to run until 2026-04-05, with start-up and commissioning activities extending through the week of 04-08. During the maintenance window, refining and downstream conversion operations will be curtailed, reducing availability of semi-finished germanium materials tied to tolling and offtake contracts. The operator indicated that feed handling and certain furnace runs will be paused first, followed by slower ramp-down of scrap melting and refining lines. It did not give a revised production target but stated that inventory buffers should cover most orders scheduled for week 03-30, while deliveries for late April are subject to confirmation. Customers were advised to revalidate shipment schedules by 2026-03-25 to align logistics with the plant restart timeline.
bodies_by_headline_hash.a5c2e208b983c7ad95f7:
Beryllium prices edged higher after exporters pointed to firmer US defense demand.  
Dealers said new procurement activity is lifting sentiment for both metal and related alloys.  
The market remains tight, with limited prompt availability reinforcing quotes in spot discussions.  
Participants noted that aerospace and military components continue to absorb supply, especially where heat-resistant and lightweight performance matters.  
Secondary supply is also less responsive than usual, reducing options for quick substitution.  
While overall demand growth looks uneven across industrial channels, defense-linked offtake is supporting near-term pricing.  
Most traders expect additional buying interest to show through in the next pricing windows, even as broader economic signals remain mixed.
bodies_by_headline_hash.e761ba627d7c73a5ef4f:
The zirconium market was quiet this week as buyers held back ahead of expected electric-vehicle (EV) orders from original equipment manufacturers, according to traders and suppliers.

Demand for zirconium products, used in nuclear fuel cladding and in some ceramics and industrial applications, has been comparatively subdued while customers wait for clearer purchasing schedules tied to battery and materials procurement. Several market participants said they are prioritising near-term inventory checks and lead-time planning, limiting activity in spot and short-term contracts.

In Asia, where zirconium-based components and feedstocks are processed for global customers, participants reported that offers remained steady but volumes were thin. “Near-term buying interest is being deferred until OEM schedules firm up,” said one metals trader, who declined to be identified because of the sensitivity of commercial orders. The trader added that sellers were still quoting levels consistent with recent assessments, but negotiations moved more slowly than in prior weeks.

Pricing in the broader zirconium complex has been shaped by expectations for downstream consumption rather than immediate physical shortages, traders said. With most market participants focused on confirming order quantities and delivery windows, liquidity has been limited. Some suppliers reported that inquiries from buyers were present but that final decisions were being delayed pending corporate procurement cycles.

Analysts have pointed to the knock-on effect of EV supply-chain planning on industrial and specialty-materials demand. While zirconium is not used directly in lithium-ion cells, it can be linked to broader industrial procurement, including manufacturing equipment, specialty ceramics and components where zirconium-based materials may be specified. Any change in the timing of large-scale equipment or component orders can ripple through procurement calendars for upstream suppliers.

Traders said they expect activity to pick up once EV OEM order books are updated and procurement teams confirm allocations to contractors. In the meantime, most deals are expected to remain focused on fulfilling existing commitments and replenishing inventories rather than expanding purchases.
bodies_by_headline_hash.164b3a5f169949dddc3e: Several lithium producers said they are targeting marginally higher volumes for the second quarter of 2026, reflecting steadier demand and improved operating availability at selected brine and hard-rock sites. In updated guidance shared in recent investor communications, companies pointed to output growth in the range of 1% to 4% quarter over quarter for Q2, after production was pressured in January and February by equipment downtime and weather-related disruptions. Industry buyers have continued to favor spot and short-term supply while negotiating longer-dated offtake terms tied to battery-grade specifications, including tighter tolerances on lithium hydroxide and carbonate purity. One group indicated ramp-up progress at a processing facility scheduled for additional commissioning in late May, while another flagged sustaining higher recoveries as stripping and evaporation stages stabilized. Deliveries are expected to remain skewed toward Asia in June, with shipments guided to rise slightly as port logistics normalize from Q1 congestion. Analysts said the guidance suggests limited near-term relief for the market, with pricing likely to hinge on offtake negotiations and conversion costs rather than pure mined volume growth.
bodies_by_headline_hash.8951f6a9812690937b1f:
Nickel miners in Indonesia are seeing higher operating costs as nickel royalties are raised.  
The change increases government take per tonne and adds cost pressure across both existing projects and expansion plans.  
Companies are already adjusting procurement and production schedules to protect margins as spending rises faster than realized prices in some segments.  
For high-cost operations, the added royalty burden can tip profitability into loss territory, prompting reviews of workforce and maintenance cycles.  
Analysts say the effect could be uneven, with better-positioned producers less exposed depending on site quality and offtake terms.  
The policy move also underscores Indonesia’s push to capture more value from downstream growth.
bodies_by_headline_hash.7e1b8f087e7444ff47e0:
Copper smelter treatment charges, or TCs, rose to multi-year highs as tighter concentrate availability lifted bargaining power for refiners and sellers began to price in improving supply conditions, according to market participants.

TCs are the fees paid by concentrate producers to smelters for processing and refining copper ore into refined metal. They are widely tracked as a barometer of the balance between concentrate supply and smelting capacity. Higher TCs typically indicate tighter concentrate markets and lower urgency for smelters to secure feedstock.

Several traders said the latest increase reflects a combination of reduced concentrate flows and delays in shipments tied to maintenance schedules at some operations, which has limited the volume of material offered into the market. At the same time, refiners have been more selective in contracting, with buyers offering shorter-duration terms and demanding clearer delivery windows, participants said.

One factor supporting the firming in TCs has been the spread between spot and contracted pricing for refined copper, which has helped sustain smelter margins and reduced the incentive to force volumes at lower fees, traders added. While some smelters have maintained production targets, they have continued to prioritize feedstock quality and logistics reliability, further tightening effective supply.

Industry sources also pointed to seasonal patterns and disruptions in upstream mining operations that have constrained concentrate output. Weather-related and industrial factors in producing regions have contributed to smaller cargoes reaching major trading hubs, they said. As a result, spot transactions have leaned toward suppliers with ready inventories, pushing TC quotes higher.

“The market is paying up for concentrate again, mainly because material availability is tighter than smelting demand,” a trader at a European metals firm said.

Despite the improvement, participants said the TC rally is likely to remain constrained by uncertainty over next-quarter supply. Some concentrate producers have deferred shipments in anticipation of better pricing, while others have sought to lock in terms before further changes in freight rates and refining schedules. Any normalization in concentrate availability could put pressure on TC levels, they warned.

Traders will watch upcoming contract negotiations and production updates from key smelting centers for signs of whether the multi-year highs can be sustained into the later months of the year.
bodies_by_headline_hash.2dcc482001de2cfb9c67: Singapore iron ore futures rebounded after a 6% weekly decline, with the most-active contract gaining on Wednesday following a late-session stabilization in global steelmaking demand expectations. The contract rose as traders reduced near-term bearish exposure after prices slid through early-week ranges, extending the pullback that began last Friday. Despite the recovery, analysts said sentiment remained fragile as China’s mill buying stayed selective and port inventories continued to fluctuate with limited prompt tightenings. Volume was thinner than the prior week, suggesting short covering rather than fresh physical-led demand. Over the week ending Monday, the contract fell about 6%, reversing part of the prior month’s gains and leaving prices still influenced by freight and exchange-rate moves. Market participants also pointed to stable downstream production schedules and ongoing policy uncertainty around property construction, factors that have kept steel output growth expectations range-bound.
bodies_by_headline_hash.bfbf889b01f511123e02:
Chinese steel mills are signaling tighter output plans for the second quarter as margins remain under pressure.  
Several operators in recent guidance tied production levels to weaker selling prices and higher cost burdens from raw materials and energy.  
The message points to a deliberate pace-down rather than a sharp cut, with maintenance and staggered restarts used to manage supply.  
Market participants expect fewer tons in the near term, especially for products most exposed to pricing competition.  
Export pressure is also a factor, with mills balancing overseas demand against domestic inventory.  
Traders will watch whether policy support and downstream consumption can stabilize profitability and extend the production discipline.
bodies_by_headline_hash.1eab05a9a8d4f32bf4b3:
Ireland’s limited impact from new incentives and policy changes under the Inflation Reduction Act (IRA) on lithium projects has left many analysts saying the law has not yet shifted supply timelines or investment decisions in a measurable way, according to industry data and interviews with supply-chain participants.

U.S. demand is forecast to keep rising as automakers expand electric vehicle output and batteries expand beyond passenger cars into grid storage and consumer electronics. But the IRA’s provisions, while designed to accelerate domestic and allied processing and to expand the availability of critical minerals, have not translated into immediate changes for upstream lithium production, several analysts said.

A central factor has been the long lead time from exploration to producing spodumene concentrate and, later, to converting it into battery-grade chemicals such as lithium hydroxide or carbonate. “The IRA can change economics on paper, but projects still have to move through permitting, financing, and offtake contracting,” one executive at a specialty mining services firm said. “That process often takes longer than policymakers’ initial horizons.”

Investors have also focused on the scale of IRA-related incentives that depend on where materials are processed and assembled. Battery makers and chemical converters have been seeking clarity on the rules around eligibility, including sourcing requirements and verification standards for critical minerals. Companies said the compliance groundwork, together with the need for long-term purchase agreements, has slowed the conversion of incentives into new construction starts.

While the IRA has spurred negotiations for new offtake deals and partnerships across the lithium value chain, analysts said the effect is more visible in contract discussions than in production volumes. Many producers and converters are still operating existing assets or are already committed to previously announced expansions that predate the IRA. Some projects that could benefit from IRA eligibility are also awaiting synchronized development of conversion capacity, which can be a bottleneck even when mining is financed.

Separately, lithium market dynamics have affected the timing of new investment. Prices have fluctuated sharply in recent quarters, and several producers have managed output to align with demand forecasts and customer contracts. That environment has made it harder for firms to justify large capital expenditures purely on the basis of future policy incentives.

Industry participants said the next measurable shift may come once conversion and refining capacity scales and more supply contracts are finalized under IRA eligibility criteria. For now, they said, the law’s influence appears concentrated in planning and contracting rather than in near-term moves in production, exports, or processed output.
bodies_by_headline_hash.4397557aa5a7aed57a49: The cobalt story remains centered on the Democratic Republic of the Congo, where much of the world’s supply is still sourced amid persistent risks spanning 2010–2026, according to industry tracking and recent compliance reporting. Demand has continued to shift toward cleaner batteries and energy storage, but the input chain has not been decoupled from artisanal and small-scale production in DRC provinces including Haut-Katanga and Lualaba. In 2023 and 2024, international buyers increased audit coverage and contract terms tied to traceability, yet auditors have cited gaps in mine-level data and shipment documentation that can take months to reconcile. Regulatory and enforcement measures in DRC have also been uneven, with periodic suspensions and quota changes affecting export flows and timing for concentrate deliveries. As pricing stays volatile and buyers rotate suppliers to meet 2025 procurement targets, analysts say the key question is whether higher due-diligence standards will translate into measurable site-level improvements rather than paperwork fixes. For now, the DRC continues to dominate supply narratives for cobalt, shaping cost, availability, and compliance outcomes for global battery makers.
bodies_by_headline_hash.ef6f38ca4232c6fa0457:
Copper has been trading less like a pure industrial metal and more like a bond proxy.  
Traders point to interest-rate dynamics as the key driver.  
When yields rise, risk appetite cools and cash flows get discounted harder, hitting copper demand expectations.  
When central banks signal easier policy, copper rebounds as growth fears ease.  
That linkage is being reinforced by positioning and liquidity flows across commodities.  
Copper also reflects broader inflation and currency moves, which can mimic macro signals usually seen in fixed income.  
So the market is watching rates and real-economy expectations at the same time.  
The result is copper acting like a macro barometer, not just a supply story.
bodies_by_headline_hash.5eaf416d6286b4f16554:
Rare earths companies and projects dominated much of the week’s headlines, while trading activity and volume skewed toward lithium, according to market participants tracking the metals complex.

Investors and analysts pointed to renewed attention on rare earths as governments and manufacturers weigh supply-chain security for elements used in magnets for electric vehicles, wind turbines and defense systems. Several updates across the sector, including progress at mining and processing ventures and fresh procurement signals, helped keep the metal group in focus, even as liquidity remained comparatively thinner than in lithium markets.

Lithium, by contrast, attracted the largest share of market attention by volume, reflecting its central role in batteries for electric vehicles and grid storage. Benchmark prices and contract negotiations for battery-grade lithium have continued to be watched closely by automakers and cathode producers, with participants noting that even small changes in demand forecasts can quickly translate into higher trading activity.

Deal activity also remained a key driver. Rare earths market coverage tended to cluster around project milestones such as feasibility studies, offtake arrangements and efforts to expand separation capacity, which can influence forward visibility for magnet supply. For lithium, the conversation more often focused on near-term production updates, concentrate and hydroxide availability, and moves by producers and refiners to manage inventory ahead of future demand.

“Rare earths can move on policy and project-specific news because of their strategic role, but lithium is where the daily trading and volumes have stayed most consistent,” said one metals trader at a bank, citing broader battery supply-chain dynamics.

Market participants said differences in how each commodity is contracted contribute to the split between headline prominence and trading volume. Rare earths are often discussed through the lens of strategic stockpiling and industrial policy, while lithium pricing and procurement tend to be tied more directly to battery demand and refinancing cycles across the sector.

Across both groups, analysts said the market’s sensitivity to China’s production and processing capacity remained a shared factor. For rare earths, China’s role in separation and magnet supply is frequently cited, while for lithium, the availability of refining output and downstream expansion continues to be central to expectations for margins and supply tightness.

In the near term, traders said they will continue monitoring policy announcements, company-specific development timelines and battery demand signals, which can shift relative interest between rare earths and lithium even as the wider energy-transition complex remains tightly linked.
bodies_by_headline_hash.d1855a7740f73b8c4acf: Ganfeng Lithium’s processing facility described in a long-form report shows how the company converts brines and spodumene feedstock into battery-grade chemicals at multiple stages, with tight monitoring of moisture, impurity levels and temperature. The site includes drying and milling lines, chemical conversion units, and purification trains that rely on solvent extraction and crystallization to remove metals such as magnesium and calcium, which can degrade cathode performance. Output is tracked by batch records tied to specific production lots and laboratory checks for lithium content, chloride residues and sulfate contamination. The report says the facility’s production flow is designed for continuous operation, but major turnarounds are scheduled around equipment inspections every 6 to 12 weeks, depending on corrosion rates and filter replacement cycles. It also details on-site utilities, including power supplies sized for peak solvent handling loads and wastewater treatment systems built to manage discharge from reagent recovery. Safety controls include gas detection, pressure relief systems and ventilation across 24/7 hazardous material zones, with incident response drills logged weekly.
bodies_by_headline_hash.c857e2ebf1e184aaf2b1:
Lithium spot prices extended a six-week pattern of shifting momentum.  
The trend stayed down-up-down, with each leg failing to break into a sustained direction.  
After a recent dip, the market briefly lifted, then slid again as bids softened.  
Deal flow remained uneven across key supply routes, with buyers showing selectivity rather than broad coverage.  
Traders cited limited urgency from downstream buyers and ongoing price testing by sellers.  
Risk premiums also appeared to fluctuate, reflecting uncertainty around near-term demand.  
Overall pricing pressure stayed mild but persistent, suggesting resistance to any clean rebound.  
Market watchers will focus on whether the next up leg holds beyond short-covering.
bodies_by_headline_hash.9db02f69e53114b91cdc:
A cobalt production and export chart tracking the Democratic Republic of Congo’s (DRC) recent outbound shipments shows the country’s export-volume moving average shifting in the most recent readings, according to data published online.

The chart, circulated through a mining and minerals information channel, plots DRC cobalt export volumes alongside a moving average designed to smooth short-term fluctuations. It uses a time series of observed export volumes and recalculates the average over a defined window, highlighting whether shipments are trending higher or lower across successive periods.

The DRC is the world’s largest source of cobalt, supplying much of the material used in lithium-ion batteries. While export volumes can move with seasonal factors, logistics and contracting cycles, the moving average is intended to give a clearer signal of underlying changes, the chart’s methodology suggests.

In the latest segment of the series shared with the chart, the moving average continues to adjust following prior swings, reflecting a gradual shift rather than a single-period spike. The visual representation indicates that the most recent export volumes are not deviating sharply from the broader trend, but are contributing to incremental changes in the averaged line.

The chart also includes points representing individual observations in the export series, which can differ from the moving average when volume data fluctuate. Analysts monitoring cobalt supply often focus on such smoothed indicators to assess whether potential disruptions—such as port congestion, changes in refinery throughput, or shifts in mining output—are beginning to affect broader export patterns.

A spokesperson for the industry monitoring channel that published the chart said the publication is intended to provide “a consistent view of shipment momentum using the moving average approach,” adding that updates reflect newly compiled export-volume figures. The channel did not provide further details about data sources, calculation windows, or whether the series is adjusted for revisions.

The DRC’s cobalt exports remain closely watched by battery supply-chain participants and trading firms because they can influence global availability and pricing. The chart’s moving-average updates offer one additional datapoint for monitoring how DRC shipment trends evolve over time.
bodies_by_headline_hash.725fe93c8ca78d0ca0eb: London Metal Exchange nickel warehouse inventories diverged from the metal’s spot price trend in late March 2026, according to market monitoring on 29 March. Data compiled from LME registered stock showed inventories moved higher over the preceding 2–4 weeks, with total holdings rising across multiple delivery points while contango in nearby contracts remained thin. Over the same period, nickel prices on the LME have been volatile, moving within a narrower daily trading range than the inventory build would typically suggest. Traders linked the mismatch to changes in regional availability, including slower warrant conversions and tighter prompt liquidity despite additional warehouse metal. The divergence also coincided with periodic shifts in forward coverage, where 3-month spreads fluctuated around marginally positive levels rather than widening sharply. Analysts said the inventory increase may reflect longer stays in bonded storage rather than a true loosening of physical supply. Further price direction was expected to hinge on whether stock levels continue rising after early April inventory reporting cycles and on demand signals from industrial offtake.
bodies_by_headline_hash.45762a9ae64c7a4e6546:
Uranium prices moved sharply lower after a major market event.  
Deal flow paused as traders digested new signals and revised near-term assumptions.  
Volatility that spiked during anticipation quickly faded into steadier pricing.  
Spot and term references both softened, suggesting limited immediate demand at current levels.  
Liquidity thinned briefly, then normalized, reinforcing a fast cooldown rather than a lasting repricing.  
Market participants shifted attention back to contract rollovers, inventory levels, and mine supply calendars.  
For now, the post-event reaction points to expectations adjusting downward, not a structural break.  
Traders are watching whether the dip attracts bids or deepens caution into the next settlement window.
bodies_by_headline_hash.e92bd312afb49cf3798c:
Rare-earth and other strategic metals prices were largely unchanged over the week, according to a commodity roundup compiled from market updates, as investors continued to weigh steady demand against muted near-term supply signals.

In the rare earth complex, most benchmark prices held in a narrow range, with declines largely offset by firmer levels in a few downstream-linked categories. The roundup showed that rare earths used in magnets and high-performance electronics did not record broad repricing, suggesting stable off-take expectations after recent contract discussions.

Among the elements most closely tracked by buyers, neodymium and praseodymium remained stable on average, while dysprosium and terbium—often associated with more volatile magnet demand—also showed little net movement for the week. Several quotes cited thin trading volumes for certain grades, limiting the extent of price discovery and helping keep week-to-week changes small.

Outside the rare earths, other commodities connected to industrial and clean-energy supply chains likewise posted minimal shifts. Metals used in refining and component manufacturing reflected steady positioning, with market participants maintaining watch on inventory levels and shipment schedules rather than making large adjustments to spot commitments.

A contributor to the compilation said the absence of significant weekly changes reflected “a wait-and-see posture as buyers monitor flows and suppliers assess contract negotiations.” The note pointed to limited new catalysts during the period, including no major disruptions to shipping or production that would typically force repricing across multiple grades.

The weekly snapshot also indicated that exchange-traded and over-the-counter rates moved in line with broader industrial sentiment, though the lack of fresh data—such as major changes in export controls, tariffs, or new offtake agreements—kept pricing range-bound. Traders said the market’s focus remained on maintaining working inventories ahead of expected demand in manufacturing cycles, rather than building large speculative positions.

Overall, the roundup described a week of consolidation across rare metals, with pricing broadly steady and traders continuing to rely on incremental supply and demand signals rather than decisive shifts in fundamentals.
